<findings>

[observation] [warning] Contains 82% of project files (36/44) — may be too broad, consider splitting
[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] General-purpose utilities (exec.ts, help-format.ts, suggest.ts, output.ts) are co-located with LLM-specific infrastructure; if these utilities are consumed by orchestration scripts rather than LLM consumers, extracting them into a separate utility zone would improve single-responsibility clarity.
[observation] [info] Perfect cohesion score (1.0) confirms all 36 files are fully interconnected within the zone — appropriate for a shared foundation package with a single public API surface.
[observation] [info] Zero coupling score reflects the architectural invariant that llm-client imports no sibling packages — it is the DAG root, as documented in public.ts.
[pattern] [warning] 86% of llm-client-core files (31/36) have no corresponding cross-zone import from the test zone, concentrating unit-test risk on the provider stack and execution layer — the most behaviorally complex parts of the package.
[relationship] [info] llm-client-core acts as the single hub for all intra-package cross-zone flows: it is consumed by llm-client-tests and shaped by package-config, with no reciprocal imports — a clean spoke-to-hub dependency direction.
[anti-pattern] [warning] CompletionRequest (types.ts) contains CLI-provider-specific fields cliFlags and timeoutMs. This shared contract type, imported by all domain packages, is contaminated with implementation details from one provider backend. CLI-specific options should move to CliProviderOptions and be excluded from the vendor-neutral request type.
[anti-pattern] [warning] The codex entry in createDefaultRegistry() constructs an inline LLMProvider adapter around createCodexCliClient rather than the codex provider implementing LLMProvider directly. Registry code is supposed to be a pure dispatch table with no provider logic; the shim belongs in codex-cli-provider.ts.
[anti-pattern] [warning] Two independent vendor selection paths exist: createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts and ProviderRegistry.getActiveProvider() in provider-registry.ts. They implement the same vendor-resolution logic (config.vendor ?? 'claude') without delegating to each other, so a new vendor must be registered in both places to be fully supported.
[anti-pattern] [warning] createLLMClient() returns ClaudeClient instead of LLMProvider. Callers of the 'vendor-neutral' factory receive a Claude-typed value, which prevents swapping vendors transparently and exposes Claude-specific interface members (e.g. mode: AuthMode) to vendor-agnostic consumers. Should return LLMProvider to honour the abstraction boundary.
[anti-pattern] [info] spawnTool() and spawnManaged() each contain a near-verbatim copy of the SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation timer block (~15 lines). Only the constant KILL_ESCALATION_MS is shared. The duplicated logic should be extracted into a private helper to prevent the two paths from drifting apart on future changes.
[suggestion] [warning] Consolidate 'llm-types.ts' and 'types.ts' into a single file or establish an explicit naming rule distinguishing them (e.g. 'types.ts' for vendor-neutral shared types, 'llm-types.ts' for provider-facing contracts); coexistence without a documented split invites accidental duplication of type definitions.
[suggestion] [warning] Rename 'api-provider.ts' → 'claude-api-provider.ts' and 'cli-provider.ts' → 'claude-cli-provider.ts' to match the 'codex-cli-provider.ts' naming pattern; all three are vendor-specific and the asymmetric naming implies api-provider/cli-provider are vendor-neutral when they are not.
[suggestion] [info] Rename 'provider-interface.ts' to 'llm-provider.ts' — the filename suffix '-interface' describes the TypeScript construct rather than the domain concept, inconsistent with every other file in the zone which names the domain entity.
[suggestion] [info] Rename 'suggest.ts' to a name that communicates its domain role (e.g. 'config-suggest.ts' or 'value-suggest.ts') — 'suggest' is too generic and does not self-document within an LLM client package.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Contains 82% of project files (36/44) — may be too broad, consider splitting
- The single-zone topology means all 36 source files are tightly interconnected by import — this is consistent with a foundation package that is designed to have high internal cohesion and zero upstream dependencies.
- The public.ts barrel re-exports the entire surface area cleanly, providing a single auditable entry point for downstream consumers (rex, hench, sourcevision, web).
- The zone mixes two distinct concerns: LLM provider infrastructure (api-provider, cli-provider, codex-cli-provider, provider-interface, provider-registry, provider-session) and general CLI/process utilities (exec, help-format, suggest, output, project-dirs). As the package grows, these could be split into sub-zones to improve discoverability.
- Perfect cohesion score (1.0) confirms all 36 files are fully interconnected within the zone — appropriate for a shared foundation package with a single public API surface.
- Zero coupling score reflects the architectural invariant that llm-client imports no sibling packages — it is the DAG root, as documented in public.ts.
- General-purpose utilities (exec.ts, help-format.ts, suggest.ts, output.ts) are co-located with LLM-specific infrastructure; if these utilities are consumed by orchestration scripts rather than LLM consumers, extracting them into a separate utility zone would improve single-responsibility clarity.
- llm-client-core is the sole hub in a hub-and-spoke topology: package-config governs its build shape, llm-client-tests consumes its exports, and no sibling packages feed into it — a clean DAG leaf from the monorepo's perspective and a clean hub from the intra-package perspective.
- Only 5 of 36 source files in llm-client-core are directly exercised by unit-test imports, leaving 86% of the zone's import surface untested at the unit level — provider, auth, exec, and session modules depend entirely on integration or e2e coverage.
- 86% of llm-client-core files (31/36) have no corresponding cross-zone import from the test zone, concentrating unit-test risk on the provider stack and execution layer — the most behaviorally complex parts of the package.
- llm-client-core acts as the single hub for all intra-package cross-zone flows: it is consumed by llm-client-tests and shaped by package-config, with no reciprocal imports — a clean spoke-to-hub dependency direction.
- createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts is typed to return ClaudeClient (Claude-specific) rather than LLMProvider (vendor-neutral), meaning the 'vendor-neutral factory' leaks the Claude type to all callers — the abstraction is incomplete at the type level.
- CompletionRequest in types.ts includes two CLI-only fields (cliFlags, timeoutMs) with doc comments acknowledging they are provider-specific — these fields pollute a shared cross-package contract type with implementation details from one provider.
- There are two independent vendor dispatch paths: createLLMClient() (llm-client.ts) and ProviderRegistry.getActiveProvider() (provider-registry.ts). Both resolve vendor → create client from LLMConfig, but they do not delegate to each other, so adding a new vendor requires updating both paths.
- The codex provider in createDefaultRegistry() wraps createCodexCliClient (which returns ClaudeClient) with an inline shim to satisfy the LLMProvider interface. The comment acknowledges this is temporary tech debt — but the shim sits inside the registry, which should contain no provider logic, blurring the registry's role as a pure dispatch table.
- spawnTool() and spawnManaged() in exec.ts duplicate the SIGTERM → SIGKILL escalation timer pattern verbatim (approximately 15 lines each). The shared constant KILL_ESCALATION_MS was extracted but the surrounding logic was not, leaving the two implementations able to diverge silently.
- exec.ts co-locates git-specific synchronous helpers (getCurrentHead, getCurrentBranch) with general process execution utilities. These are domain-specific operations that couple the process module to git semantics.
- createLLMClient() returns ClaudeClient instead of LLMProvider. Callers of the 'vendor-neutral' factory receive a Claude-typed value, which prevents swapping vendors transparently and exposes Claude-specific interface members (e.g. mode: AuthMode) to vendor-agnostic consumers. Should return LLMProvider to honour the abstraction boundary.
- CompletionRequest (types.ts) contains CLI-provider-specific fields cliFlags and timeoutMs. This shared contract type, imported by all domain packages, is contaminated with implementation details from one provider backend. CLI-specific options should move to CliProviderOptions and be excluded from the vendor-neutral request type.
- Two independent vendor selection paths exist: createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts and ProviderRegistry.getActiveProvider() in provider-registry.ts. They implement the same vendor-resolution logic (config.vendor ?? 'claude') without delegating to each other, so a new vendor must be registered in both places to be fully supported.
- The codex entry in createDefaultRegistry() constructs an inline LLMProvider adapter around createCodexCliClient rather than the codex provider implementing LLMProvider directly. Registry code is supposed to be a pure dispatch table with no provider logic; the shim belongs in codex-cli-provider.ts.
- spawnTool() and spawnManaged() each contain a near-verbatim copy of the SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation timer block (~15 lines). Only the constant KILL_ESCALATION_MS is shared. The duplicated logic should be extracted into a private helper to prevent the two paths from drifting apart on future changes.
- Naming asymmetry across provider files: 'api-provider.ts' and 'cli-provider.ts' have no vendor prefix while 'codex-cli-provider.ts' carries an explicit vendor prefix — implying api-provider and cli-provider are Claude-specific by convention but not by name, which misleads readers about vendor scope.
- 'create-client.ts' uses verb-object (action) naming while every other file in the zone uses noun naming (api-provider, auth, config, exec) — the factory module breaks the implicit naming convention of the package.
- 'suggest.ts' is an opaque name inside an LLM client package; without reading the file, it is unclear whether it provides LLM prompt suggestions, CLI autocomplete, or something else — the name does not self-document its role.
- 'provider-interface.ts' uses a TypeScript concept ('interface') as a filename suffix rather than the domain concept it represents; conventional TypeScript naming would be 'provider.ts' or 'llm-provider.ts', reserving the file for the type definition rather than advertising its implementation form.
- 'llm-types.ts' and 'types.ts' coexist in the same zone, creating ambiguity about which file owns which type contracts — without reading both, consumers cannot determine the difference between 'llm types' and generic 'types', risking duplication or misrouting of new type definitions.
- Rename 'api-provider.ts' → 'claude-api-provider.ts' and 'cli-provider.ts' → 'claude-cli-provider.ts' to match the 'codex-cli-provider.ts' naming pattern; all three are vendor-specific and the asymmetric naming implies api-provider/cli-provider are vendor-neutral when they are not.
- Consolidate 'llm-types.ts' and 'types.ts' into a single file or establish an explicit naming rule distinguishing them (e.g. 'types.ts' for vendor-neutral shared types, 'llm-types.ts' for provider-facing contracts); coexistence without a documented split invites accidental duplication of type definitions.
- Rename 'suggest.ts' to a name that communicates its domain role (e.g. 'config-suggest.ts' or 'value-suggest.ts') — 'suggest' is too generic and does not self-document within an LLM client package.
- Rename 'provider-interface.ts' to 'llm-provider.ts' — the filename suffix '-interface' describes the TypeScript construct rather than the domain concept, inconsistent with every other file in the zone which names the domain entity.
- [call graph] 443 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 51 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
